speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE] [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Is there a [INAUDIBLE] on my face?

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good. I know when I have a pimple on my forehead. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: No, I think you look really very handsome.

speaker: interviewer
text: And roll.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, this is tape number one with Sol Liber, Saul, S-A-U-L.

speaker: interviewee
text: S-O-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: S-O-L, excuse me. Liber, L-I-B-E-R.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Merle Goldberg, and I'm the interviewer. Today is August 11.

speaker: interviewer
text: 1994, Thursday. And we are in Beverly Hills, California. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] OK. First, can you tell me where and when you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born on December 3, 1923 in the city of Grojec.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is Grojec?

speaker: interviewee
text: Grojec is 52 kilometers from Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell me something about your life in your village or--

speaker: interviewee
text: --city.

speaker: interviewer
text: City. Prior to the war, what your life was like growing up.

speaker: interviewee
text: Prior to the war, it was a quiet city. We had 5,200 Jewish inhabitants in the city. We had one cinema. We-- my father's occupation was he leased out orchards from farmers. We usually get it, the fruit, and took it to market to Warsaw. And for the fall, we-- the fruit that was, we were able to hold for the winter, we kept it in cellars. And through the winter, we picked out the ripe ones and took it to the market. My older two brothers were tailors. I finished two and a half years apprentice as a tailor till the war. And one of my sisters was a machine operator in shirts. ' older sister took care of the family in the house, because my mother was sick always. And the youngest one was at school. And there wasn't much exciting in the city, as far as I, as I feel. And what-- that's about all I could tell you, unless you have more questions.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think I do. If you could tell me your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Sana Liber and my mother's name and maiden name was Shayndel Gromon.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why don't you tell me something about Jewish life in your town, in Grojec?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish life was like anywhere else in Europe. The people were mostly observant people, about 90% I could say. Jewish life, holidays, observed the Sabbath, went to shul, kept kosher, most of them. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the Jewish elder were involved in the city government, some of them. Some of them were involved in Jewish life-- committees for-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they had a committee for burial, they had a committee to help poor people. And this was-- people were, was, were occupied in the businesses. A lot of people had the stores, liquor stores. There was one big one because you had to have permission from the government. And there were grocery stores, restaurants. Then there was also a market day, was Thursday, that people-- the farmers used to bring the products to sell. And people used to put up stands to sell suits, shoes, all necessities for people's everyday life. And this was the city of Grojec.

speaker: interviewer
text: And for you, what was Jewish life like with your family for example?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- kept a kosher house, my mother did. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were not Hasidic. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My father attended shul every Saturday-- Friday night, Saturday, holidays-- but not every day. We had no chance, actually. If you prayed, you prayed at home. Because we were preoccupied making a living. Most of the religious people had businesses that they kept on Saturdays closed, so they were able to observe holidays more than-- although if we had to go to Warsaw with the horses and wagon to deliver fruit, we didn't drive on Saturday. We didn't ride on Saturday. We send somebody non-Jewish to take it, the load, to Warsaw. And this, this was the life, this-- as I, as I remember it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like with your brothers and sisters as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, we had no quarrels like, as I remember. We were-- we were a tight family. We had to be tight, we only lived in a two bed-- two small bedrooms and a kitchen. There were no living rooms or dens or-- this, only a millionaire could afford a house like-- an apartment like this. And you have to be today, a millionaire to afford it too. [LAUGHS] Anyway, we were very close. Everybody went about as business, went to work. And whoever stayed home was housecleaning, cooking, and preparing for the, for the men to come home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience any sort of anti-Semitism? What kind of anti-Semitism did you experience before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it started, like, when Hitler came to power. He planted the fifth column, what they call it, you know. They had a lot of Volksdeutsche what they call Germans born in Poland. These were the easiest people for him to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] organize. And they start walking around with the calling, don't buy from Jews. This started about 1937, '36. And it was a problem. But actually in Poland, there was always anti-Semitism, to be honest. And-- because school, we had a-- we went to public school, but separated. Jewish children knows only. The only time when I went with Polish kids to school was evening school. It was a must. When you went to learn a trade, you dropped out before finished seven grades. You had to go to evening school. And you also had to belong to a military unit training. So when you're called up to the army, you, you saved six months of your service. Instead of serving two years, you served a year and a half.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is the only connection. It wasn't easy. But until the late, the late '30s, before the, before the war, you were not afraid to go out. You could have gone anywhere in the city.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was more free to go than I'm here, you see. You walked at night, there was no problem. There were incidents but not visible, until around 1936, 1937.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in 1936, 1937, you were talking about the Volksdeutsche. So can you explain to me those first signs of danger?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, actually, they weren't Volksdeutsche alone. Yellow There were Polish people also that were organized in groups. You know, it's very easy to organize, don't buy from Jews. Like you saw it happened in Germany, at Kristallnacht and all the-- you see, so-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] All the European countries as I see them now that if you were born there and lived-- Jews lived in Grojec since the 1700s, if I'm not mistaken. I have an article here. And I have a Jewish encyclopedia. But still they don't consider you-- in the first couple hundred years, you didn't have any rights, until about 1850 or something. And then they gave people rights to own land and businesses and everything. But you always felt a stranger. A matter of fact, I feel a stranger wherever I go.

speaker: interviewer
text: As strange as you felt then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it's, it's in you, when you went through what I went through, it's-- wherever you go, unless you go to Israel, I feel, I feel free. I feel like I belong, you know. It's no, it's no hiding that. That's what it is. It's the honest truth.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when these first signs of danger, what, what-- could you explain to me those first signs of danger, and what went through your mind, your family's mind, and take me through that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, everybody thought that it, it will pass. Because people weren't used to-- it used to be free, you were on the market to sell stuff, nobody bothered you, you know. But then a couple years before the war, it start really getting pesty, you know. And I was young. And I was not involved in the market to sell, so I didn't feel the-- but I saw it. But I didn't feel the way the people felt when they had to sell merchandise, you see. But then it's-- it was all till the war came around and the real mess came, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why don't you take me from that point forward then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when the, the German declared war, it took him only a few days [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to come to our city. First the city was bombed. The boat, the flour mills were taken out. And my uncle got killed in the first flour mill. His name was Ishmuel Schultz. And they bombed the city at random, just to make chaos, you know. And then they came in. The first thing they did is take out 12 Jews that belonged to the city government and the elder, they shot him in the middle, in the marketplace. This was the first [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] taste of the German brutality. And then a lot of us escaped. We were in, actually four kilometers in our orchards that we were gathering the fruit for the fall already. And the Germans came in-- we heard the news about what happened in the city. And I, actually I was always the one that took the initiative. And I went into the city, and I saw what's going on. The next day they came in. And they parked the tanks under the trees. They were still fighting for Warsaw. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And one-- a day or two after they parked the tanks under the trees in the orchards, they, they were pushed back from Warsaw by the Polish defenders a few kilometers. So they picked up men, from 15 to 50, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] picked me up and my father. And they took us to the city jail, kept us overnight. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You know rumors were going around that maybe they'll mine the, the jail or whatever. Next morning, they marched us out, six wide, about 3,500 people, Poles and Jews. They marched us with-- in a dirt road, to a city, Biala, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] without water, without food. If you run for a little water that people used to hand out, you were shot. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] On the way, we lost about three, four dozen people. Some people couldn't walk. It was about 22, 23 kilometers. They were riding half, half-- half-tracks in the front, and half in the back. And we were in the middle. You know, it's easier to ride a half-track than to work. Nobody was used to walk 22, 23 kilometers in one day, unless you were in the army. We came-- yeah, we had a watchman in the city that repaired watches. He was a heavy, actually Hasidic Jew. And he couldn't keep up. They tied him-- they tied a chain around his waist and tied him to the half track. And they tried-- he should walk, but he couldn't. So he fell, and that's how he died, by pulling him on the ground. They made pictures and were laughing, the Germans, you know. And eventually, we came into a big ranch. They took us into a big ranch. And they told us to sit down on our legs, the same way we marched to sit on floor with. And they brought us some ice. Because they were afraid if they give us water, we could all get sick. So they brought us pieces of ice to everybody, no food. And all of a sudden we heard some firing. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Light machine gun fire. You know, everything was new. I never heard in my life shooting from a machine gun. And the rumors was going around that they shot four people that tried to escape. See, everybody knew around the surroundings, 22 kilometers from the city. So they shot-- four people were shot, four they brought back. They put up a gallow. And we had to see them hang all of them four. This was my first experience as a 15-year-old boy. And next, we stayed overnight, just sitting like this our whole night. The morning we got a slice of bread and a little bit of black coffee. The bread that was left over from the Polish army. It was stale. And they marched us, also the same distance probably, from Biala to Rawa, Rawa Mazowiecka. We came Rawa, it was getting dark. They pushed us in a church that probably could take 500 people, they pushed in 3,000. Because we lost on the way a lot of people. The group was getting smaller. The same rumor started. They gonna mine it, they're going to burn it. It was so hot and there was no place to move. You stayed like sardines, you know. That we took our chance, we broke windows in the church, to get some air. And was the same story, the stench, you know, no facilities. Next morning, they marched us out of the-- out of Rawa. We went through the city of Lodz. We went through the city of Zdunska Wola, until we arrived to the city of Sieradz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at Sieradz, they took us-- Sieradz was the biggest jail of the whole country of Poland. There's some big criminals, like here, what do they call it in San Francisco? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Rock.

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed overnight there. This is without food, just water. In the morning, they marched us out. They gave us the same Polish way-- a slide of bread and coffee, from the Polish Army, leftover, in jail there. And they put us in-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they put us in the cattle cars. And they took us to Breslau. This is the closest city to Poland. In Breslau, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we found out that Warsaw fall, fell. So we saw the Red Cross. From the Red Cross, they were wearing armbands. From the Red Cross, women came and brought some fresh bread and cheese and coffee.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they told us to get out, to clean out the cars. And we ate this portion of food. And they load us back, and they took us back to Sieradz. You want to ask a question? Well, I wanted to know, when you left Rawa and they marched you through Lodz, did they pick Jews up everywhere you went?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they picking more Jews up everywhere?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, only this group.

speaker: interviewer
text: Only this group, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us back. We traveled the whole night. We came back to Sieradz. They marched us out on a road. They told us that they're going to release, they're going to count off 20 people at a time and tell us to run, and they're going to shoot. If somebody doesn't run fast, if he, he will be shot. Well, I wasn't. I was always marching. It's easiest way to march, if you're on the first four. You set the, the-- how do you say it, the pace. If you-- no matter how fast you walk, if you're behind, you always fall behind. Well, that's what, that's what they said, that's what they did. They counted off 20 people, said run. They just opened fire. A few fell. My father was hit in the thigh. And I pushed my father in the-- there were ditches on both sides of the road. And I jumped in, I told him to walk. Bend down and walk, you know. And we walked away. And then let's say, I don't know how far we went, and he was bleeding. It went through the skin, the skin, the bullet. So I tear off a piece of my shirt and I tied it around. And I held him up, and we walked. We walked away, and we saw a village. We walked into the village, and I told them my father was shot. And they gave me some real gauze, one lace around. And I cleaned it out, and asked for some iodine. I figured at least it wouldn't get infected. And I put some iodine and I tied it around. And I made him like a crutch, you know, to be able to walk. And it took us about seven, eight days to walk home. Because the roads were crowded with, with army units, going back and forth, you know. And you also had to walk not on the main road, because you were afraid you're going to be picked up for work. That's what some did. They came to Lodz, and some people were picked up and held for weeks for work. When they came home they told us-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we came, we came home-- it was about the time of Sukkot, you know. The city was deserted. There was a-- they were already taking people to work. Well, when we came home, we couldn't recognize the city. My parents-- first of all, they confiscated everything. They told us to get out of the orchards, they took whatever they wanted. They took over the whole ranch, with everything. Horses, wagons, they needed everything. But also the family came to the city. Came to the city, they started taking people to work. The first work I encountered was they took me to spread manure with my hands. This was like [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not considered work. It was like to-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] can't find the word for it. To downgrade us, let's say.

speaker: interviewer
text: To break your spirit.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the best-- To break your spirit. This is the best I could figure out. Well, we worked a few days on this. Then they took us to other work, to load, to unload. You know, it's a war. Passed a couple of months, the winter came. The winter came, they start taking us to-- this is no pay. No food, no pay. The parents used to bring us some food, cooks they still had some-- everybody had some money yet, so it lasted for a while. They took us to, to clean the roads. They had no plows. They had plenty of people, plenty of Jews to clean.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, everybody needed a shovel. How many shovels did a person have in a house? He had one or two.

speaker: interviewee
text: And if you took four or five people, they make you shovel-- clean the snow with your hands. This is the truth. Clean the roads, the snow, with your hands. You came home-- you cleaned the roads, you went out about 10, 15 kilometers. When you were walking back, the wind blew the snow back to the road. You walked in the snow, up to your knees. Before your clothes dried, in the morning, 4 o'clock, they took you again to work. This went on until they start bringing in, they told us to stay in one area. It wasn't actually like closed ghetto like with the police or SS. But they told us-- they start bringing in people from villages, from small towns. Ours was considered a big city, as cities go in Poland. And in the same, meantime, they took over the post office. And the SS moved in, for a torture chamber. If they found you were on a train or on a bus that you went somewhere as a Jew, they took you there, and you never came out. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this was going on through the winter. When the summer came, the rumor was starting that we're going to go to Warsaw Ghetto. This was already from 1939. And I'm thinking if I didn't skip anything. Yeah, they formed a Judenrat in the city. They usually picked people that are not any better than the Germans. And, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and if you were a friend of theirs, they tried-- they got you out of the working situation. If not, they picked the poor people or the people that didn't care for them, they made them work, you know. On different-- you worked for the gendarmes, you worked for the SS, you worked-- in every place that they could spare people to go on the front. And they'd put people to work, instead of using soldiers. Well, this was going on, like I said, to the, till-- in meantime, the Warsaw Ghetto was formed. And they start putting people from all over. And we were told we're going to move to the Warsaw Ghetto. This was beginning of 1940. And they told us to prepare-- the Judenrat told us to prepare. Put in everything in suitcases, you couldn't take any furniture. You could only take what you could carry. And they tricked us to put everything together. Then when we had to go to the ghetto, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they took us into this post office, where the SS was staying. They took everything away, beat the hell out of us, and told us to go. When we came into the Warsaw Ghetto, we were already beaten, like downtrodden, you know. And we came into the ghetto, you saw already people dying in the street. It was not a nice picture, you know. If you put half a million people in the ghetto, and you don't give them-- people that went to work, they took Kommandos. They're called the Kommando, they took 200 people. They marched them out, so at least they got food where they worked on the airfield, building airfields, and unloading on the train loads, you know, with ammunition. All kind of things.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the people that were in the Ghetto, what can you do? There's no work. The only rations they gave you, even a child couldn't survive. Why don't we hold on here?
